title: Coupled phase transitions in crystalline solids with extreme chemical disorder
authors: Subha Dey, Rukma Nevgi, Suresh Chandra Joshi, Sourav Chowdhury, Nandana Bhattacharya, Kashish Kapoor, Tinku Dan, Subhadip Chowdhury, Sabyasachi Karmakar, S. D. Kaushik, Shibabrata Nandi, Christoph Klewe, Manuel Valvidares, Moritz Hoesch, George E. Sterbinsky, Srimanta Middey
abstract: Structural phase transitions often couple to magnetic and electronic degrees of freedom, enabling emergent phenomena in solids. In high-entropy oxides (HEOs), which typically stabilize in highly symmetric cubic phases, such transitions are considered rare due to the extreme chemical disorder-analogous to the behavior observed in high-entropy alloys. This raises a fundamental question: can the rich physics of coupled phase transitions persist in such disordered systems? Here, we show that targeted design of compositionally complex oxides (CCOs) can trigger symmetry-lowering transitions, with spinel-type materials serving as a representative case. For instance, [Mn$_{0.2}$Co$_{0.2}$Ni$_{0.2}$Cu$_{0.2}$Zn$_{0.2}$]Cr$_2$O$_4$, having two Jahn-Teller (J-T) active ions, undergoes two successive coupled structural transitions upon cooling: an orbital-driven transition at 100 K and a magnetism-driven transition at 40 K. Systematic substitution of $A$-site cations reveals that both Ni and Cu are essential for these transitions. Element specific local structure investigations uncover distinct and opposing local distortions around Ni and Cu, while Mn, Co, and Zn remain largely undistorted. These results establish that CCOs can host coupled phase transitions through `cooperation via competition' among local distortions in a chemically disordered lattice. This discovery expands the design principles for complex oxides, introducing a new paradigm for tuning structural and functional properties in high-entropy systems beyond conventional symmetry constraints.
summary: This paper shows that extreme chemical disorder does not necessarily suppress phase transitions in high-entropy oxides. By using Jahn-Teller active ions, the researchers achieved coupled structural and magnetic transitions driven by the competition between local distortions. This provides a new paradigm for tuning properties in complex, disordered materials.
main_problem: Determining whether the rich physics of coupled structural, magnetic, and electronic phase transitions can persist in highly chemically disordered high-entropy oxides (HEOs).
main_result: The authors demonstrate that targeted design of compositionally complex oxides can trigger symmetry-lowering transitions through 'cooperation via competition' between opposing local distortions from Jahn-Teller active ions.
method: The study uses solid-state synthesis, powder X-ray diffraction (XRD), EXAFS for local structure, X-ray absorption spectroscopy (XAS), XMCD, and magnetic/heat capacity measurements.
model_or_system: A series of compositionally complex chromate spinels with the general formula [A]Cr2O4, specifically focusing on the highly disordered [Mn0.2Co0.2Ni0.2Cu0.2Zn0.2]Cr2O4.
key_observables: Structural phase transitions (cubic to tetragonal to orthorhombic), magnetic ordering (ferrimagnetism), bond lengths, Debye-Waller factors, and heat capacity peaks.
important_parameters: Jahn-Teller active ions (Ni2+ and Cu2+), configurational entropy (S_config), and transition temperatures (100 K and 40 K).
main_assumptions: The study assumes the chemical distribution is homogeneous at the micrometer and nanometer scales.
figures_summary: Figure 1 compares phase transitions in elemental vs. high-entropy systems; Figure 2 shows XRD-based phase evolution; Figure 3 presents EXAFS data on local bond length changes; Figure 4 displays magnetic susceptibility, magnetization loops, XMCD, and heat capacity data.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of disorder-induced suppression of transitions, presents the synthesis and characterization of the complex spinel, details the structural and magnetic phase evolution, explains the local mechanism via Jahn-Teller distortions, and concludes with the implications for oxide design.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Structural transitions in elemental solids and chromate spinels. a, Representative phase transitions in elemental solids as a function of temperature at ambient pressure. [7–11] b, High entropy alloys, containing five elements A, B, C, D, E, generally adopt either FCC, BCC, or HCP structures and do not show structural phase transition upon cooling. c, Crystal structure of a normal spinel AB2O4, showing the tetrahedral A-site and octahedral B-site coordination environments. d, Structural and magnetic phase transitions in individual ACr2O4 spinels (A = Mg, Mn, Co, Ni, Cu, Zn), illustrating a progression from cubic paramagnetic (PM) to symmetry-lowered and magnetically ordered phases,...

FIG. 2. Structural transitions and orbital ordering. a, The evolution of the selected Bragg peaks upon lowering the temperature. The splitting of the Bragg peak below 100 K indicates a structural phase transition. The fitting of the Bragg peak around 2θ ∼57◦requires three peaks at 60 K and four peaks at 20 K. b, XRD refinement at 60 K (cubic + tetragonal) and 20 K (tetragonal + orthorhombic). The inset shows a zoomed view of the XRD refinement of the peak with the highest intensity. * denotes the aluminum sample holder XRD peak. c Schematic of two different types of tetragonal distortion in spinel oxides. d9 system favors tetrahedra compression, but d8 system favors tetrahedra elongation to...

FIG. 3. Temperature-dependent variation of local structural distortions probed by EXAFS. Fourier transformed EXAFS spectra along with fittings at 150 K and 10 K, obtained at the a Ni K and b Cu K edges. c, Schematic describing the first neighbor A-O and the second neighbor A-Cr bond length from A site [A = Mn, Co, Ni, Cu, and Zn]. The variation of bond lengths in the first coordination shell of d, J-T active Ni and Cu and e, non-J-T active Mn, Co, and Zn cations. The variation of bond lengths in the second coordination shell of f, J-T active Ni and Cu and g, non-J-T active Mn, Co, and Zn cations. Variation of mean squared displacement σ2 in the second neighbor (A-Cr) bond distances in h J-T...

Fig.3d,e present the first-neighbor A-O bond distances ob- tained from EXAFS fitting for J-T active and inactive cations, respectively. These distances exhibit a pronounced depen- dence on the identity of the A-site ion, even at a fixed temper- ature. Most notably, the Ni-O and Cu-O bond lengths show subtle but distinctly opposing deviations below 100 K, which is more pronounced below 50 K, coinciding structural transi- tions observed in X-ray diffraction. A more pronounced an- tagonistic behavior is observed in the second-neighbor Ni-Cr

The hysteresis between FCC and FCW indicating magneto-structural transition. b, Field-dependent ZFC magnetization at 5 K measured upto ±14 T magnetic field. c, Temperature-dependent AC susceptibility at 9777 Hz frequency showing characteristics peak around 40 K for both the real and imaginary part.
